MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUEST, DEMAND INSPECTION, PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS
Off Calendar Apr-26-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSE TO REQUEST, DEMAND INSPECTION, PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
SAMUEL F. IZZO, of PARK LLP, (213) 616-7438, for Plaintiff.
No other appearances.

Not argued/contested. The court notes that it did not receive notice of an appearance to object to the tentative ruling. The court confirms that counsel did not notify opposing counsel regarding appearing today in accordance with the tentative ruling. Counsel advises that he was not able to view the tentative ruling until this morning. The court advises that the tentative rulings were posted yesterday and confirms that the tentative rulings were indeed viewable.

After additional discussion, the court adopts its tentative ruling. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant University of San Francsico's Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Production is GRANTED. The California Constitution guarantees all people certain inalienable rights, among them the right of "pursuing and obtaining . . . privacy." Cal. Const., art. 1, Sec. 1. The right of privacy extends to information in an individual's medical records, information about his or her physical and mental condition, and information in his or her medical history. See John B. v. Superior Court, 38 Cal. 4th 1177, 1198-99 (2006); Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn., 7 Cal.4th 1, 41 (1994). "The right to privacy, however, is not absolute." John B. v. Superior Court, 38 Cal. 4th 1177, 1199 (2006). In appropriate circumstances, this right must be balanced against other important interests. See Hill v. National Collegiate Athletic Assn., 7 Cal. 4th 1, 37 (1994). "On occasion [a party's] privacy interests may have to give way to [the] opponent's right to a fair trial. Thus, courts must balance the right of civil litigants to discover relevant facts against the privacy interests of persons subject to discovery." Vinson v. Superior Court, 43 Cal. 3d 833, 842 (1987). Here, Plaintiff admitted that all of the medical care received at San Francisco General Hospital was related to the incident in question. To that end, those records requested in RFP No. 1 and No. 2 are ORDERED to be produced without redaction. Naturally, this Order should not in any way prejudice Plaintiff's right to move to exclude any irrelevant or highly prejudicial information contained in the records at any evidentiary proceeding or at trial. The requests for sanctions are DENIED.

There is a request from Defense counsel to extend the deadline to produce records referenced in the tentative ruling to next Friday, May-03-2024. Counsel's request is granted by the court. The court advises that it will update the proposed order accordingly. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem at pvanzandt@aghwlaw.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Peter Van Zandt. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (302/JPT)
